articles.title: WHY IS THERE ALWAYS A MEETING BOT IN YOUR ZOOM CALL?
→ Get started with $100 in free credits
SPRITES ON THE WEB
articles.summary: In 2015, Twitter used image sprites to create its animated "Like" button, a technique borrowed from video games to run complex animations on low-end devices. Sprites work by combining all frames of an animation into a single image, called a spritesheet, then using CSS properties like `object-fit: cover` and `object-position` within an img tag to display each frame sequentially. The `steps()` timing function in CSS is necessary for advancing through these frames discretely, avoiding smooth transitions and creating a stop-motion effect.

articles.title: DICTIONARY COMPRESSION IS FINALLY HERE, AND IT'S RIDICULOUSLY GOOD
articles.summary: Dictionary compression is a newly widely supported technique for sending data over the web. It works by initializing the compression algorithm with a pre-shared dictionary of known data, allowing subsequent transmissions to only send references to this dictionary rather than the full content. This approach shrinks frequently updated content like JavaScript bundles, WebAssembly files, and API responses.

articles.title: WRITING CODE IS CHEAP NOW
articles.summary: Agentic engineering changes software development by making code writing cheaper. This shift impacts everything from macro-level project planning to micro-level decisions about refactoring, testing, and documentation. While delivering basic code is now nearly free, delivering good code, defined by its quality, reliability, simplicity, and maintainability, is still hard.

articles.title: WHAT I'VE LEARNED FROM SHIPPING 25+ MOBILE APPS
articles.summary: Most Rails teams overestimate how much native code they actually need. Most of what users do and need is standard CRUD that works great as server-rendered HTML in a native wrapper. The apps that struggle are the ones that go native too early on too many screens, tripling their maintenance burden for no real user benefit.

articles.title: IS AGE VERIFICATION A TRAP?
articles.summary: Age-verification laws create an age-verification trap by having platforms implement intrusive data collection practices that undermine user privacy. To prove compliance, platforms must gather and indefinitely store sensitive personal data, including government IDs, biometric scans, and behavioral logs. This data retention directly conflicts with modern privacy laws, which advocate for collecting only necessary data for defined purposes and storing it minimally.

articles.title: CREATE AN AGENT MVP IN 30 DAYS
articles.summary: Go from idea to production‐ready agent quickly. This developer checklist [12] shows how to use Microsoft Foundry to build and ship an agent MVP in ~30 days, with practical guidance on architecture, performance, and tradeoffs to inform early technical decisions.

articles.title: PGDOG
articles.summary: PgDog is a high-performance proxy for PostgreSQL, written in Rust, designed to scale databases by offering connection pooling, load balancing, and advanced sharding capabilities. It parses SQL queries to manage connections, distribute reads and writes, handle failovers, and route queries across multiple shards.

articles.title: INTRODUCING UPRIGHT: AN OPEN SOURCE SYNTHETIC MONITORING SYSTEM
articles.summary: Upright is an open-source synthetic monitoring system built as a Rails engine. It deploys to global VPS nodes via Kamal and conducts various probes, including advanced Playwright browser checks, HTTP, SMTP, and Traceroute, from multiple geographic locations.

articles.title: WHAT I LEARNED DESIGNING A (BAREBONES) UI ENGINE
NODE.JS VS DENO VS BUN PERFORMANCE BENCHMARKS
articles.summary: Bun won eight tests across 14 microbenchmarks on an M4 Mac (especially HTTP throughput and large JSON parsing), Deno won five (async scheduling and tight arithmetic), and Node won just one (small-buffer SHA-256 hashing). Node stayed competitive overall but didn't dominate anywhere.

articles.title: SIGNALS: TOWARD A SELF-IMPROVING AGENT
articles.summary: Factory built a system that uses LLMs to review thousands of coding agent sessions daily, spotting where users got frustrated. For example, frustrations would manifest themselves as rephrasing the same request five times or fighting with context windows. When enough friction is found, the agent files its own bug ticket, writes a fix, and opens a PR for a human to approve.

articles.title: FULLY REMOTE)
articles.summary: As the first engineer on TLDR's new Applied AI team, you'll build AI agents and composable Claude Skills to let non-technical teammates create their own AI workflows. Learn more [19].

articles.title: DETECTING AND PREVENTING DISTILLATION ATTACKS
articles.summary: Anthropic has detected industrial-scale "distillation attacks" by three AI labs - DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax - who illegally extracted Claude's capabilities through fraudulent accounts.

articles.title: MAKING WOLFRAM TECH AVAILABLE AS A FOUNDATION TOOL FOR LLM SYSTEMS
articles.summary: Wolfram Tech is a "foundation tool" that supplements LLM systems with precise, deep computation and knowledge.

articles.title: CODE ISN'T SLOWING YOUR PROJECT DOWN, COMMUNICATION IS
articles.summary: Poor communication, rather than code complexity, is the primary factor that slows down software projects and shapes system architecture.
